codeSnapshot.label: long_term_memory.py
codeSnapshot.code:
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI, OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_chroma import Chroma
from langchain_core.messages import SystemMessage, HumanMessage

llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o", temperature=0)
embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")

# --- 1. Initialise the long-term memory store ---
# ChromaDB persists to disk so memories survive across sessions.
vectorstore = Chroma(
    collection_name="agent_long_term_memory",
    embedding_function=embeddings,
    persist_directory="./chroma_memory",
)

# --- 2. Store memories ---
# In production, this happens at session end after LLM-based extraction.
memories_to_store = [
    "User prefers concise, bullet-pointed answers over long paragraphs.",
    "User is a software engineer at a fintech company.",
    "User likes coffee and often works early in the morning.",
    "User is currently studying for ML system design interviews.",
    "User uses Python and prefers type hints in all code examples.",
]

vectorstore.add_texts(
    texts=memories_to_store,
    metadatas=[
        {"category": "preference", "session": 1},
        {"category": "profile",    "session": 1},
        {"category": "personal",   "session": 1},
        {"category": "goal",       "session": 1},
        {"category": "preference", "session": 1},
    ],
)
print(f"Stored {len(memories_to_store)} memories in ChromaDB.")

# --- 3. Retrieve relevant memories at session start ---
user_query = "Can you help me practise a system design interview question?"

relevant_memories = vectorstore.similarity_search(user_query, k=3)
print("\nTop-3 retrieved memories:")
for i, mem in enumerate(relevant_memories, 1):
    print(f"  {i}. {mem.page_content}")

# --- 4. Inject retrieved memories into the prompt ---
memory_block = "\n".join(
    f"- {mem.page_content}" for mem in relevant_memories
)

system_prompt = f"""You are a helpful AI assistant with memory of past interactions.

Known facts about this user:
{memory_block}

Use this context to personalise your responses."""

# --- 5. Call the LLM with memory-augmented context ---
response = llm.invoke([
    SystemMessage(content=system_prompt),
    HumanMessage(content=user_query),
])

print("\nAgent response (memory-augmented):")
print(response.content)

# --- 6. After the session: extract and store new memories ---
# In production, run an extraction prompt over the full conversation.
new_memory = "User struggled with estimating QPS in the rate-limiter design question."
vectorstore.add_texts(
    texts=[new_memory],
    metadatas=[{"category": "learning_gap", "session": 2}],
)
print("\nNew memory stored for next session.")

eli3: Long-term memory for AI agents is stored outside the model — in a database, a file, or a vector store — and can be retrieved across multiple sessions. Unlike short-term memory (the context window, which disappears when the session ends), long-term memory persists forever until explicitly deleted.

Imagine an assistant who, after your first conversation, writes down key facts about you in a notebook: your name, your preferences, past tasks you've done together. Next time you talk, they read the notebook first. That notebook is long-term memory.

Technically, LLMs have no intrinsic long-term memory — they're stateless. All long-term memory must be explicitly managed by the application: store information after a session ends, retrieve relevant facts before the next session starts, and inject them into the system prompt or context.

whenWhy.title: Personalisation across sessions
whenWhy.body: Without long-term memory, every session starts from scratch — the agent doesn't know the user's name, preferences, or past interactions. With long-term memory, the agent retrieves relevant user facts at the start of each session: 'This user prefers concise responses, works in fintech, previously asked about CAPM.' This enables genuine personalisation. Production personal AI assistants (Mem, Notion AI, GitHub Copilot Workspace) all implement some form of long-term user memory.

whenWhy.title: Persisting knowledge from completed tasks
whenWhy.body: Agents that perform research, analysis, or multi-session projects need to remember what they've already done. Long-term memory stores completed sub-tasks, discovered facts, and made decisions so future sessions can build on them rather than restarting from scratch. Example: a research agent that has already found and summarised 20 papers stores those summaries; the next session retrieves them and continues with papers 21-40, avoiding redundant work.

whenWhy.title: Agent identity and behavioral continuity
whenWhy.body: Long-term memory enables consistent agent behavior over time: preferences, past mistakes, established conventions, and ongoing commitments. An agent without long-term memory can't learn from its mistakes across sessions; one with it can avoid repeating errors it has been corrected on. This is why agent memory architectures often distinguish between episodic memory (what happened) and semantic memory (what was learned from what happened).

deepDiveIntro: Long-term memory for LLM agents is implemented entirely outside the model itself — LLMs are stateless functions that produce no side effects beyond their output tokens. Persistence requires an external storage system: typically a vector database (Pinecone, Weaviate, pgvector) that stores memories as dense embeddings, enabling semantic retrieval. At session start, the agent embeds the current query and issues a cosine similarity search to retrieve the top-k most relevant memories, which are then injected into the system prompt or early context. At session end, new information is extracted from the conversation and written back to the store as new memory entries. This retrieval-augmented pattern decouples memory capacity from context window size — the store can hold millions of memories while only the most relevant handful enter any given context.

The memory lifecycle has four distinct phases: (1) Capture — extract memorable information from the session, either explicitly ('Remember that...') or implicitly via LLM-based extraction; (2) Consolidation — deduplicate, resolve conflicts between old and new facts, and compress redundant entries; (3) Retrieval — semantic similarity search at session start to surface relevant memories; (4) Forgetting — memories decay by age, are explicitly deleted, or are superseded by newer, higher-confidence entries. This lifecycle mirrors the human memory system and was formalised in the MemGPT architecture (Packer et al. 2023), which treats the agent as an operating system managing its own virtual memory across main context and a backing store.

deepDive.heading: Storage Backends for Long-Term Memory
deepDive.body: Three common backends: (1) Vector database (Pinecone, Weaviate, pgvector) — stores memories as embeddings, enables semantic retrieval. Best for unstructured, natural-language memories. Retrieval: cosine similarity search over embedded memories; (2) Key-value store (Redis, DynamoDB) — stores structured facts as key-value pairs. Best for explicit facts: user_name, user_timezone, user_preferences. Retrieval: exact lookup by key; (3) Relational database (PostgreSQL) — stores structured, queryable memories. Best for memories with complex relationships: completed tasks, established conventions with timestamps. Retrieval: SQL queries. Production systems often combine all three: vector DB for semantic retrieval, KV for profile facts, SQL for structured history. [1]

deepDive.heading: Memory Extraction and Consolidation
deepDive.body: Two approaches to memory extraction: (1) Explicit — user says 'Remember that I prefer Python over JavaScript'. The agent detects the command and stores the fact immediately; (2) Implicit — the LLM reviews the conversation at session end and extracts memorable facts autonomously. Implicit extraction requires careful prompting: 'Extract up to 5 key facts from this conversation that would be useful to remember for future sessions. Output as JSON array of {fact, category, confidence}.' Consolidation: before storing, check for duplicates (embedding similarity > 0.95) and conflicts (if a new fact contradicts a stored one, update the stored memory and log the change). [2]

deepDive.heading: MemGPT and the Virtual Context Architecture
deepDive.body: MemGPT (Packer et al. 2023) models long-term memory as a hierarchical memory system inspired by OS virtual memory: 'main context' (in-context, fast, limited) and 'external storage' (out-of-context, slow, unlimited). The agent has explicit tools: recall_memory(query) to retrieve from external storage, save_memory(fact) to write to external storage. The agent manages its own memory — deciding what to save and when to retrieve — as part of the agent loop. This self-directed memory management allows the agent to maintain coherence across conversations far longer than the context window. [2]

deepDive.heading: Memory Consolidation and Forgetting Strategies
deepDive.body: Unbounded memory growth degrades retrieval precision: a store with 100k entries returns noisier top-k results than one with 1k curated entries. Three forgetting strategies counteract this: (1) Time-based decay — assign each memory an expiry or a decaying relevance score; memories not retrieved in the last 90 days are demoted to cold storage or deleted; (2) Supersession — when a new memory contradicts a stored one (detected via embedding similarity + semantic conflict check), mark the old entry as superseded rather than deleting it, preserving an audit trail; (3) Reflection-based consolidation — periodically prompt the LLM to review clusters of related memories and merge them into a single, higher-confidence canonical fact, discarding the individual entries. This mirrors human memory consolidation during sleep. The result is a memory store that remains compact, consistent, and high-precision over long agent lifetimes. Combine with a changelog so superseded memories can be recovered if needed. [1][2]

deepDive.heading: Memory Consolidation and Deduplication
deepDive.body: As a long-term memory store accumulates entries across sessions, two structural problems emerge: redundancy (multiple memories express the same fact with slight paraphrasing) and contradiction (a newer memory conflicts with an older one). Both degrade retrieval quality — redundant entries consume top-k slots with nearly identical content, while contradictory entries confuse the model when both are retrieved simultaneously. Deduplication before storing is the first line of defence: embed the candidate memory, compute cosine similarity against existing memories, and reject or merge the new entry if similarity exceeds a threshold (typically 0.90–0.95). Merging is preferable to rejection: a small LLM call can synthesise the two phrasings into a single canonical statement, preserving any nuance from both. Consolidation addresses the longer-term problem: periodically cluster all memories by embedding similarity, then prompt the LLM to merge each cluster into one high-confidence canonical fact. This is the approach MemGPT's memory management layer employs — the agent itself is responsible for calling archival_memory_search to find related memories before calling archival_memory_insert, so it can detect and consolidate duplicates in real time. For contradiction detection, a classification prompt ('Does statement B contradict statement A? Answer yes/no with reasoning.') applied to high-similarity pairs catches semantic conflicts that cosine similarity alone misses. [1][2]

deepDive.heading: Privacy and Memory Expiry
deepDive.body: Any system that stores user-specific memories across sessions is subject to data privacy regulations, most critically GDPR (EU) and CCPA (California). GDPR's right to erasure (Article 17) requires that a user's memory records can be fully deleted on request — meaning every memory entry must be linked to a user ID and the deletion pipeline must propagate to all storage backends (vector store, cold archive, backup). Time-to-live (TTL) policies address the data minimisation principle (Article 5): memories should not be retained longer than necessary for the declared purpose. A practical TTL scheme: preference memories expire after 12 months of inactivity, task-specific memories expire after 30 days, and profile facts (name, role) persist until explicitly deleted. User-initiated memory deletion — a 'forget what you know about me' feature — must be surfaced in the product UI and must trigger hard deletion, not just soft-flagging. Anonymisation is an alternative to deletion for aggregate analytics: strip user identifiers from memories before archiving for model improvement. Finally, memory content should be encrypted at rest (AES-256) because it contains PII, and access logs should be maintained to demonstrate compliance. Building these controls into the architecture from the start is far cheaper than retrofitting them after a compliance audit. [1]

takeaways: Long-term memory is stored externally (vector DB, KV store, SQL) and retrieved at session start — not intrinsic to the model. [1]
Memory lifecycle: capture → consolidate (deduplicate, resolve conflicts) → retrieve (semantic search) → forget. [2]
Semantic retrieval: embed current context, cosine-search stored memories, inject top-k into system prompt. [1]
MemGPT: agent manages its own memory with explicit save/recall tools — self-directed memory management. [2]
Combine backends: vector DB for unstructured facts, KV for user profile, SQL for structured history. [1]

edgeCases.q: How do you handle conflicting memories? (e.g., the user previously said they prefer Python, now says they prefer TypeScript)
edgeCases.a: Conflict resolution strategies: (1) Last-write-wins — the newer memory supersedes the older one; simple but loses history; (2) Temporal versioning — store both memories with timestamps; at retrieval time, retrieve the most recent version of each fact; (3) Explicit conflict detection — at save time, embed the new memory, find similar stored memories (cosine > 0.85), and if a conflict is detected, flag it: 'You previously remembered X; should I update it to Y?' Letting the user confirm avoids silent data loss; (4) Maintain a changelog — never delete a memory, mark it as superseded: {fact: 'prefers Python', superseded_by: 'prefers TypeScript', date: ...}. The changelog enables auditing and rollback.

edgeCases.q: How much long-term memory context is safe to inject without degrading LLM performance?
edgeCases.a: Empirically: inject at most 5-15 relevant memories per session (500-2000 tokens). Injecting more memories: (1) increases the probability of including irrelevant ones that confuse the model; (2) consumes token budget needed for the actual conversation; (3) triggers the lost-in-the-middle effect if memories are too numerous. Best practice: use high-precision retrieval (k=5 with a relevance threshold) rather than high-recall (k=50). Place retrieved memories in a structured 'User Memory' block in the system prompt, not inline in the conversation. For memories with different categories (preferences, past tasks, profile facts), use separate retrieval queries to ensure diversity.

sources.label: Packer et al. (2023) — MemGPT: Towards LLMs as Operating Systems
Wang et al. (2024) — A Survey on Large Language Model based Autonomous Agents
